response:
Short answer: because the remediation flow had to move off the browser into a backend/server flow and Fleet/APIM expect/validate Rackspace-style X-Auth-Tokens (with the caller classified as a “racker” and carrying the platform roles). Entra client‑credentials were either unsuitable to the new backend flow (and unsafe in the browser) and didn’t produce the same Rackspace/X‑Auth semantics or tenant/DDI behavior the Fleet/APIM stack needs, so we switched the backend to mint tokens from Rackspace Identity instead.

Key reasons (condensed):
- Security/architecture: the call was moved from browser→Fleet to backend→Fleet. You can’t safely use Entra client secrets in a browser; a backend flow needs an identity token that Fleet/APIM will accept for application-mediated remediation.
- Compatibility with APIM/Fleet: APIM’s auth logic and Fleet expect Rackspace X-Auth-Token semantics (X-User-Roles, caller classification as racker) and validate that token in APIM policies. Entra client creds don’t produce the same X-Auth behavior, so using Rackspace Identity aligns with existing APIM policy validation and role classification.
- Tenant/DDI and operational quirks: Entra token flows didn’t match platform expectations around tenant/DDI for operator/Racker accounts (not returning the same x-tenant-id/DDI behavior), which caused failures in the Event Grid remediation plumbing. Rackspace Identity produces the tokens/headers the downstream services expect.
- Simpler permissions/ops: Using Rackspace Identity avoids adding fragile Entra application registrations/permissions across tenants and fits with the existing governance/policy-engine and backend-py implementations.

These points motivated the 2026-04-09 revision to acquire Event Grid remediation tokens from Rackspace Identity instead of using Entra client credentials.
